title: Writing - Part 1: Animals in Captivity
description: Your class has been discussing whether keeping animals in zoos and wildlife parks is ethical.

content.question: Write an essay discussing two of the arguments in your notes. You should explain which argument you find most persuasive, giving reasons for your opinion.

arguments: Animal welfare and quality of life
opinions: Zoos save species from extinction that would otherwise disappear.
No matter how large the enclosure, it can never replace an animal's natural habitat.
Seeing real animals helps people care more about conservation.

structure.introduction: Introduce the debate about keeping animals in captivity and the two arguments you will discuss.
structure.bodyParagraph1: Discuss the first argument (e.g., conservation of endangered species) and its importance.
structure.bodyParagraph2: Discuss the second argument (e.g., animal welfare) and the ethical concerns it raises.
structure.conclusion: Evaluate both arguments and state which you find most persuasive.

content.modelAnswer:
Zoos and wildlife parks have existed for centuries, but their role in modern society continues to be debated. While some see them as vital conservation centres, others argue that keeping animals in captivity is fundamentally wrong. This essay will consider the case for species conservation and the question of animal welfare.

Modern zoos play an important role in protecting endangered species from extinction. Many animals, including the giant panda and the Arabian oryx, owe their survival partly to captive breeding programmes. Without these initiatives, several species would likely no longer exist. Zoos also contribute to scientific research that helps protect animals in the wild.

However, opponents argue that captivity inevitably compromises an animal's quality of life. Even in the best-designed enclosures, animals cannot behave as they would in their natural environment. Many develop signs of stress and psychological distress, which raises serious ethical questions about whether any human benefit can justify this suffering.

In conclusion, while the conservation work carried out by zoos is genuinely valuable, I believe animal welfare must take priority. If an animal's physical and psychological needs cannot be fully met in captivity, then keeping it there is difficult to justify. Greater investment in protecting wild habitats would ultimately be a more ethical and effective way to preserve biodiversity.
